schools.summary: God's renewed charge not to fear, with the promise that Ai was given into Joshua's hand: 'and the LORD said unto Joshua, Fear not, neither be thou dismayed: take all the people of war with thee, and arise, go up to Ai: see, I have given into thy hand the king of Ai, and his people, and his city, and his land' — the restoration after defeat — 'and the LORD said unto Joshua, Fear not, neither be thou dismayed: take all the people of war with thee, and arise, go up to Ai: see, I have given into thy hand the king of Ai, and his people, and his city, and his land': after the defeat and the purging of Achan's sin, God renewed his charge and his promise (the witnesses noting the significance — the gracious restoration of God's people after sin and chastisement, the renewal of the promise once the sin was put away, Israel, defeated at Ai because of Achan, now forgiven and recommissioned, the fear that the defeat bred answered by God's renewed word, the lesson — that God restores his people after their fall, removing the cause of his anger and renewing his promise of victory, the encouragement 'fear not' after the shame of defeat, the second chance of grace, the truth that God does not cast off his people for ever, but after chastening restores them, 'though he fall, he shall not be utterly cast down: for the LORD upholdeth him with his hand,' Psalm 37:24, the figure — the renewal of grace after repentance, God lifting up his people who had fallen, 'if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins,' 1 John 1:9, the truth that, the sin purged, God gives the victory freely, 'I have given into thy hand,' the conquest his gift, the figure of the second chance, the restored commission, the encouragement of the cast-down, the call to courage grounded on the renewed promise of God, the warning that defeat comes by sin, but grace restores the penitent, the figure of the restoration of God's people after defeat and the renewal of his promise); God renewing the charge to courage and the advance on Ai after the defeat, the fear answered (the restoration after sin, Psalm 37:24) — the charge (fear not, go up); 'see, I have given into thy hand the king of Ai, and his people, and his city, and his land' (the promise of victory renewed, the city given freely now the sin was purged, the conquest God's gift, the renewal of grace after repentance, 1 John 1:9) — the figure (the second chance, the restored commission, the providence, the promise, the leave to take the spoil to follow, vv.2) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the renewed charge 'fear not, I have given Ai into thy hand' establish?

sides.position: The restoration of God's people after defeat and the renewal of his promise - after the defeat and the purging of Achan's sin, God renews his charge and promise of victory, the encouragement 'fear not' after the shame of defeat; God does not utterly cast off his people but restores the penitent (Psalm 37:24; 1 John 1:9), giving the conquest freely now the cause of his anger was removed

themes: And the LORD said unto Joshua, Fear not, neither be thou dismayed: take all the people of war with thee, and arise, go up to Ai: see, I have given into thy hand the king of Ai, and his people, and his city, and his land
God's renewed charge not to fear, with the promise that Ai was given into Joshua's hand (the crux of the restoration of God's people after defeat)
the gracious restoration of God's people after sin and chastisement, the renewal of the promise once the sin was put away, the encouragement 'fear not' after defeat
God who does not utterly cast off his people but restores the penitent and gives the conquest freely (Psalm 37:24; 1 John 1:9)

schools.summary: That Ai was to be treated as Jericho, but the spoil might be taken: 'and thou shalt do to Ai and her king as thou didst unto Jericho and her king: only the spoil thereof, and the cattle thereof, shall ye take for a prey unto yourselves: lay thee an ambush for the city behind it' — Ai was to be destroyed like Jericho, but the spoil was now allowed, and an ambush set (the and thou shalt do to Ai and her king as thou didst unto Jericho and her king, Ai destroyed like Jericho, the only the spoil thereof, and the cattle thereof, shall ye take for a prey unto yourselves, the spoil allowed, the lay thee an ambush for the city behind it, the ambush, the figure, Ai under the ban as Jericho, but the spoil now given, the contrast with the devoted Jericho, the truth that Jericho, the firstfruits, was wholly devoted, but Ai's spoil was granted, the firstfruits to God and the rest to the people, the figure of the firstfruits given wholly to God and the increase enjoyed, 'the firstfruit... is holy,' Romans 11:16, the spoil allowed where Achan had sinned, the lawful enjoyment of what God grants, the ambush appointed, the conquest by God's plan, vv.3); Ai destroyed like Jericho but the spoil allowed (the firstfruits and the increase, Romans 11:16) — the spoil (taken for a prey); 'lay thee an ambush for the city behind it' (the ambush appointed, the conquest by God's plan, the figure, the conquest ordered by God, the providence, the spoil and ambush, the men sent by night to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt do to Ai and her king as thou didst unto Jericho and her king: only the spoil thereof, and the cattle thereof, shall ye take for a prey unto yourselves: lay thee an ambush for the city behind it
that Ai was to be treated as Jericho, but the spoil might be taken
Ai destroyed like Jericho, but the spoil now granted where Achan had sinned
the firstfruits wholly devoted and the increase lawfully enjoyed (Romans 11:16)

schools.summary: Joshua choosing thirty thousand men and sending them by night: 'so Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up against Ai: and Joshua chose out thirty thousand mighty men of valour, and sent them away by night' — Joshua mustered the army and sent a force by night to lie in ambush (the so Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up against Ai, the army arising, the and Joshua chose out thirty thousand mighty men of valour, and sent them away by night, the ambush force sent by night, the figure, the leader acting promptly on God's word, the ambush force sent under cover of night, the truth that Joshua obeyed at once, ordering the conquest as God directed, the figure of the wise use of means in the work of God, the ambush a lawful stratagem, the army mustered, the force sent by night, the conquest carried out by God's plan, vv.2); Joshua mustering the army against Ai (the advance) — the muster (the army arising); 'Joshua chose out thirty thousand mighty men of valour, and sent them away by night' (the force sent by night to ambush, the stratagem, the figure, the wise use of means in God's work, the providence, the muster, the instructions for the ambush to follow, vv.4-8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: So Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up against Ai: and Joshua chose out thirty thousand mighty men of valour, and sent them away by night
Joshua choosing thirty thousand men and sending them by night
Joshua mustering the army and sending a force by night to lie in ambush
obeying God's word and ordering the conquest as he directed

schools.summary: The ambush charged to lie in wait behind the city and be ready: 'and he commanded them, saying, Behold, ye shall lie in wait against the city, even behind the city: go not very far from the city, but be ye all ready' — the ambush was to hide behind the city, near and ready to strike (the and he commanded them, saying, Behold, ye shall lie in wait against the city, even behind the city, the ambush behind the city, the go not very far from the city, but be ye all ready, near and ready, the figure, the ambush placed near and ready, the stratagem ordered, the truth that the force was to be at hand, ready to take the city the moment it was left, the figure of the readiness required in the work of God, the men at their post, the ambush near the city, the plan in place, the orderly carrying out of the stratagem, vv.3); the ambush set behind the city (the stratagem) — the ambush (behind the city); 'go not very far from the city, but be ye all ready' (the force near and ready, the readiness, the figure, the readiness in God's work, the providence, the charge, Joshua to draw the men of Ai out to follow, vv.5-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he commanded them, saying, Behold, ye shall lie in wait against the city, even behind the city: go not very far from the city, but be ye all ready
the ambush charged to lie in wait behind the city and be ready
the ambush to hide behind the city, near and ready to strike the moment it was left
the stratagem ordered and the men at their post

schools.summary: Joshua's plan to draw the men of Ai out by feigned flight: 'and I, and all the people that are with me, will approach unto the city: and it shall come to pass, when they come out against us, as at the first, that we will flee before them' — Joshua would advance and then feign flight, as in the first defeat, to lure the men of Ai out (the and I, and all the people that are with me, will approach unto the city, the advance, the and it shall come to pass, when they come out against us, as at the first, that we will flee before them, the feigned flight, the figure, the stratagem of the feigned flight, the men of Ai drawn out by their former success, the truth that the very defeat that shamed Israel was turned into the bait of the ambush, the figure of the wisdom that turns past failure to advantage, the enemy lured by their pride, the feigned flight that draws the foe from the city, the stratagem that opens the city, vv.6); Joshua advancing on the city (the approach) — the advance (approach the city); 'when they come out against us, as at the first, that we will flee before them' (the feigned flight to lure the foe, the stratagem, the figure, the wisdom that turns failure to advantage, the providence, the plan, the men of Ai drawn from the city to follow, vv.6-7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And I, and all the people that are with me, will approach unto the city: and it shall come to pass, when they come out against us, as at the first, that we will flee before them
Joshua's plan to draw the men of Ai out by feigned flight
Joshua advancing and then feigning flight, as in the first defeat, to lure the men of Ai from the city
the very defeat that shamed Israel turned into the bait of the ambush

schools.summary: That the men of Ai would be drawn out, thinking Israel fled as before: '(for they will come out after us) till we have drawn them from the city; for they will say, They flee before us, as at the first: therefore we will flee before them' — the men of Ai, supposing Israel fled as before, would pursue and be drawn from the city (the (for they will come out after us) till we have drawn them from the city, the foe drawn out, the for they will say, They flee before us, as at the first: therefore we will flee before them, the foe deceived by the former defeat, the figure, the men of Ai deceived by their pride, drawn from the city, the truth that the enemy's confidence in their first victory became their snare, the figure of the pride that goes before a fall, the foe lured by overconfidence, 'pride goeth before destruction,' Proverbs 16:18, the city emptied by the lure, the stratagem that succeeds by the enemy's presumption, vv.5); the foe drawn from the city (the lure, Proverbs 16:18) — the drawing out (from the city); 'they will say, They flee before us, as at the first: therefore we will flee before them' (the foe deceived by the former defeat, the pride that snares, the figure, the pride that goes before a fall, the providence, the lure, the ambush to rise and take the city to follow, vv.7-8) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: (For they will come out after us) till we have drawn them from the city; for they will say, They flee before us, as at the first: therefore we will flee before them

schools.summary: The ambush to rise and seize the city, for God would deliver it: 'then ye shall rise up from the ambush, and seize upon the city: for the LORD your God will deliver it into your hand' — the ambush was to rise and take the open city, God delivering it (the then ye shall rise up from the ambush, and seize upon the city, the ambush rising to seize the city, the for the LORD your God will deliver it into your hand, God giving the city, the figure, the ambush rising to take the emptied city, the victory God's gift, the truth that the stratagem succeeded because God delivered the city, the means and the gift together, the figure of the use of means crowned by God's gift, the conquest his, 'the LORD your God will deliver,' the city seized by the ambush, God's hand in the win, the means and the promise joined, vv.8); the ambush rising to seize the city (the stratagem) — the seizure (rise and seize); 'the LORD your God will deliver it into your hand' (God delivering the city, the victory his gift, the figure, the use of means crowned by God's gift, the providence, the seizure, the charge to set the city on fire to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge to fire the city as God commanded: 'and it shall be, when ye have taken the city, that ye shall set the city on fire: according to the commandment of the LORD shall ye do. See, I have commanded you' — the city was to be set on fire when taken, as God commanded (the and it shall be, when ye have taken the city, that ye shall set the city on fire, the city to be fired, the according to the commandment of the LORD shall ye do. See, I have commanded you, by God's command, the figure, the city fired as the signal and the judgment, by God's command, the truth that the burning was to be done as God commanded, the signal to the rest, the figure of the obedience that does all by God's word, the city fired as ordered, the smoke the signal of the taken city, the judgment carried out by command, vv.7); the city to be set on fire when taken (the signal and judgment) — the firing (the city on fire); 'according to the commandment of the LORD shall ye do' (the firing by God's command, the obedience, the figure, the obedience that does all by God's word, the providence, the charge, the ambush sent and Joshua lodging with the people to follow, vv.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The ambush sent and Joshua lodging among the people: 'Joshua therefore sent them forth: and they went to lie in ambush, and abode between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of Ai: but Joshua lodged that night among the people' — the ambush was deployed between Bethel and Ai, while Joshua stayed with the main force (the Joshua therefore sent them forth: and they went to lie in ambush, and abode between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of Ai, the ambush deployed, the but Joshua lodged that night among the people, Joshua with the people, the figure, the ambush in place, the leader with the main force, the plan set, the truth that the stratagem was deployed as ordered, Joshua remaining with the host, the figure of the orderly carrying out of the plan, the parts in place, the ambush hidden, the leader ready, the conquest prepared, vv.4); the ambush placed between Bethel and Ai (the deployment) — the deployment (the ambush set); 'Joshua lodged that night among the people' (Joshua remaining with the main force, the plan set, the figure, the orderly carrying out of the plan, the providence, the deployment, Joshua rising and numbering the people to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Joshua rising early and leading the people up to Ai: 'and Joshua rose up early in the morning, and numbered the people, and went up, he and the elders of Israel, before the people to Ai' — Joshua rose early, mustered the people, and led them up to Ai (the and Joshua rose up early in the morning, and numbered the people, the early muster, the and went up, he and the elders of Israel, before the people to Ai, leading the people up, the figure, the diligent leader rising early and leading the advance, the elders with him, the truth that Joshua led the people himself, going before them, the figure of the leader who goes before his people in the work, the host arrayed, the advance to Ai, the leader at the head, the conquest begun, vv.9); Joshua rising early and numbering the people (the muster) — the muster (numbered the people); 'went up, he and the elders of Israel, before the people to Ai' (Joshua leading the people up, the leader at the head, the figure, the leader who goes before his people, the providence, the advance, the army camping north of Ai to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joshua rose up early in the morning, and numbered the people, and went up, he and the elders of Israel, before the people to Ai
Joshua rising early and leading the people up to Ai

schools.summary: The army camping north of Ai with the valley between: 'and all the people, even the people of war that were with him, went up, and drew nigh, and came before the city, and pitched on the north side of Ai: now there was a valley between them and Ai' — the army camped on the north of Ai, a valley between them and the city (the and all the people, even the people of war that were with him, went up, and drew nigh, and came before the city, the army before the city, the and pitched on the north side of Ai: now there was a valley between them and Ai, camped north, the valley between, the figure, the army in position before the city, the valley between, the plan in place, the truth that the main force took its station to draw the foe out, the figure of the orderly disposition of the host, the position taken, the army north of Ai, the ambush west, the trap set, the conquest arrayed, vv.9); the army coming before the city (the position) — the advance (before the city); 'pitched on the north side of Ai: now there was a valley between them and Ai' (the army camped north, the valley between, the trap set, the figure, the orderly disposition of the host, the providence, the position, the ambush west and Joshua in the valley to follow, vv.12-13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: A second ambush of five thousand set on the west: 'and he took about five thousand men, and set them to lie in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city' — a second ambush of five thousand was set on the west between Bethel and Ai (the and he took about five thousand men, the five thousand, the and set them to lie in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city, the ambush on the west, the figure, a further ambush set to complete the trap, the city ringed, the truth that the plan was reinforced, the city to be taken on both sides, the figure of the thoroughness of the plan, the ambush doubled, the city surrounded, the trap complete, the conquest arrayed, vv.9); the second ambush of five thousand (the reinforcement) — the force (five thousand); 'set them to lie in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city' (the ambush set on the west, the trap complete, the figure, the thoroughness of the plan, the providence, the ambush, Joshua going into the valley to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The host arrayed and Joshua going into the valley by night: 'and when they had set the people, even all the host that was on the north of the city, and their liers in wait on the west of the city, Joshua went that night into the midst of the valley' — the host was arrayed north and the ambush west, and Joshua went into the valley by night (the and when they had set the people, even all the host that was on the north of the city, and their liers in wait on the west of the city, the host arrayed, the Joshua went that night into the midst of the valley, Joshua in the valley, the figure, the host in array, the ambush in place, the leader in the valley, the trap complete, the truth that all was set for the conquest, Joshua himself going forward by night, the figure of the leader who places himself in the work, the plan in motion, the army north, the ambush west, the leader in the valley, the conquest ready, vv.12); the host arrayed north and the ambush west (the disposition) — the array (north and west); 'Joshua went that night into the midst of the valley' (Joshua going into the valley by night, the leader in the work, the figure, the leader who places himself in the work, the providence, the array, the king of Ai seeing and going out to battle to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The king of Ai going out to battle, unaware of the ambush: 'and it came to pass, when the king of Ai saw it, that they hasted and rose up early, and the men of the city went out against Israel to battle, he and all his people, at a time appointed, before the plain; but he wist not that there were liers in ambush against him behind the city' — the king of Ai hastily went out to fight, not knowing of the ambush behind him (the and it came to pass, when the king of Ai saw it, that they hasted and rose up early, the king hastening out, the and the men of the city went out against Israel to battle, he and all his people, the sally, the but he wist not that there were liers in ambush against him behind the city, the ambush unknown, the figure, the king sallying out in haste, blind to the ambush behind him, the truth that the foe rushed to his ruin, ignorant of the trap, the figure of the blindness of those marked for judgment, the snare unseen, 'they know not, neither will they understand,' Psalm 82:5, the king drawn out by his confidence, the ambush hidden, the foe rushing to destruction, vv.16); the king hastening out to battle (the sally) — the sally (out against Israel); 'he wist not that there were liers in ambush against him behind the city' (the ambush unknown, the foe blind to the trap, the blindness before judgment, Psalm 82:5, the figure, the blindness of those marked for judgment, the providence, the sally, the feigned flight drawing the foe from the city to follow, vv.15-17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And it came to pass, when the king of Ai saw it, that they hasted and rose up early, and the men of the city went out against Israel to battle, he and all his people, at a time appointed, before the plain; but he wist not that there were liers in ambush against him behind the city

schools.summary: Joshua and Israel feigning flight before the men of Ai: 'and Joshua and all Israel made as if they were beaten before them, and fled by the way of the wilderness' — Joshua and Israel pretended to be beaten and fled, drawing the foe after them (the and Joshua and all Israel made as if they were beaten before them, the feigned defeat, the and fled by the way of the wilderness, the flight, the figure, the feigned flight that lured the foe, the stratagem in action, the truth that the pretended defeat drew the men of Ai out, the trap springing, the figure of the wisdom that feigns weakness to overcome, the foe deceived, the flight that opened the city, the stratagem succeeding, vv.5-6); Israel pretending to be beaten (the stratagem) — the feigned defeat (made as if beaten); 'fled by the way of the wilderness' (the flight that drew the foe out, the lure, the figure, the wisdom that feigns weakness to overcome, the providence, the flight, all the people of Ai pursuing to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: All Ai called out to pursue, drawn away from the city: 'and all the people that were in Ai were called together to pursue after them: and they pursued after Joshua, and were drawn away from the city' — all the men of Ai were called out to chase Israel and so were drawn from the city (the and all the people that were in Ai were called together to pursue after them, all called to pursue, the and they pursued after Joshua, and were drawn away from the city, drawn from the city, the figure, the whole city emptied in the pursuit, the trap closing, the truth that the foe, drawn out to a man, left the city open, the figure of the snare that empties the stronghold, the pursuit that ruins, the city left defenceless, the ambush ready, the foe drawn to destruction, vv.15); all Ai called out to pursue (the emptying) — the pursuit (all called out); 'they pursued after Joshua, and were drawn away from the city' (the foe drawn from the city, the trap closing, the figure, the snare that empties the stronghold, the providence, the pursuit, Bethel also drawn out and the city left open to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That all of Ai and Bethel went out, leaving the city open: 'and there was not a man left in Ai or Bethel, that went not out after Israel: and they left the city open, and pursued after Israel' — every man of Ai and Bethel went out in pursuit, leaving the city undefended (the and there was not a man left in Ai or Bethel, that went not out after Israel, all went out, the and they left the city open, and pursued after Israel, the city left open, the figure, the city wholly emptied, left open to the ambush, the trap sprung, the truth that the foe, deceived, left the city undefended, ripe for the taking, the figure of the judgment that strips the defences of the doomed, the city open, the foe drawn out to a man, the city defenceless, the conquest at hand, vv.16); every man of Ai and Bethel gone out (the emptying) — the emptying (not a man left); 'they left the city open, and pursued after Israel' (the city left undefended, the trap sprung, the figure, the judgment that strips the defences of the doomed, the providence, the open city, God's word to stretch out the spear to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's command to stretch out the spear toward Ai, the signal of the victory God would give: 'and the LORD said unto Joshua, Stretch out the spear that is in thy hand toward Ai; for I will give it into thine hand. And Joshua stretched out the spear that he had in his hand toward Ai' — the signal of faith — 'and the LORD said unto Joshua, Stretch out the spear that is in thy hand toward Ai; for I will give it into thine hand': Joshua was to stretch out his spear as the signal that God would give the city (the witnesses noting the significance — the leader's act of faith and obedience that is at once the signal of the victory and the token of God's giving, the spear stretched out the sign to the ambush and the pledge of God's promise, 'I will give it into thine hand,' the lesson — that the victory is God's gift, the leader's act the appointed means and signal, not the cause, the spear held out in faith, the conquest God's work, the truth that this answers to Moses's outstretched hand at the Red Sea and over Amalek, the leader's gesture the channel of God's power, 'when Moses held up his hand, that Israel prevailed,' Exodus 17:11, the figure — the act of faith that becomes the instrument of God's deliverance, the means God appoints joined to his gift, the truth that God works his victories through the obedient acts of his servants, the spear the visible token of the unseen hand, the figure of the outstretched hand that signals and conveys the power of God, fulfilled in the prayer and faith of the church that prevails, the call to act in faith on God's word, the gesture and the gift together, the warning that the means without God avail nothing, but God's gift is given through the means, the figure of the leader's act of faith that is the signal and instrument of God's deliverance); God commanding Joshua to stretch out the spear as the signal to the ambush and the token of the victory (the signal of faith, Exodus 17:11) — the command (stretch out the spear); 'for I will give it into thine hand. And Joshua stretched out the spear that he had in his hand toward Ai' (the promise 'I will give it into thine hand' joined to Joshua's obedient act, the victory God's gift through the appointed means, as Moses's hand over Amalek, the act of faith the channel of God's power, Exodus 17:11) — the figure (the outstretched hand that signals and conveys God's power, the providence, the act, the ambush rising and firing the city to follow, vv.19) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Joshua's outstretched spear signify?

sides.position: The leader's act of faith that is the signal and instrument of God's deliverance - at once the signal to the ambush and the token of God's giving 'I will give it into thine hand,' the means God appoints joined to his gift, the victory his work not the gesture's, answering to Moses's outstretched hand at the Red Sea and over Amalek (Exodus 17:11), a figure of the act of faith and prayer that becomes the instrument of God's power

themes: And the LORD said unto Joshua, Stretch out the spear that is in thy hand toward Ai; for I will give it into thine hand. And Joshua stretched out the spear that he had in his hand toward Ai
God's command to stretch out the spear toward Ai, the signal of the victory God would give (the crux of the leader's act of faith that is the signal and instrument of God's deliverance)
the leader's act of faith and obedience that is at once the signal of the victory and the token of God's giving, the means God appoints joined to his gift
answering to Moses's outstretched hand at the Red Sea and over Amalek, a figure of the act of faith that becomes the instrument of God's deliverance (Exodus 17:11)

schools.summary: The ambush rising at the signal, taking and firing the city: 'and the ambush arose quickly out of their place, and they ran as soon as he had stretched out his hand: and they entered into the city, and took it, and hasted and set the city on fire' — at Joshua's signal the ambush rose, took the open city, and set it on fire (the and the ambush arose quickly out of their place, and they ran as soon as he had stretched out his hand, the ambush rising at the signal, the and they entered into the city, and took it, and hasted and set the city on fire, the city taken and fired, the figure, the ambush springing at the signal, the city taken and fired, the plan fulfilled, the truth that the signal was obeyed at once, the city seized, the figure of the prompt obedience that seizes the moment, the work done, the city taken and fired, the smoke the sign, the conquest accomplished, vv.18); the ambush rising at the signal (the obedience) — the rising (at the signal); 'they entered into the city, and took it, and hasted and set the city on fire' (the city taken and fired, the plan fulfilled, the figure, the prompt obedience that seizes the moment, the providence, the seizure, the men of Ai trapped between the two forces to follow, vv.20-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the ambush arose quickly out of their place, and they ran as soon as he had stretched out his hand: and they entered into the city, and took it, and hasted and set the city on fire
the ambush rising at the signal, taking and firing the city
at Joshua's signal the ambush rose, took the open city, and set it on fire
the prompt obedience that seized the moment and accomplished the conquest

schools.summary: The men of Ai seeing the smoke and trapped, with Israel turning back: 'and when the men of Ai looked behind them, they saw, and, behold, the smoke of the city ascended up to heaven, and they had no power to flee this way or that way: and the people that fled to the wilderness turned back upon the pursuers' — the men of Ai saw the smoke of their burning city and were trapped, as Israel turned to fight (the and when the men of Ai looked behind them, they saw, and, behold, the smoke of the city ascended up to heaven, the smoke of the burning city, the and they had no power to flee this way or that way, trapped, the and the people that fled to the wilderness turned back upon the pursuers, Israel turning to fight, the figure, the foe trapped between the burning city and the turning army, the snare closed, the truth that the men of Ai, seeing their loss, were caught between two forces, the figure of the ruin that closes on the doomed, no way of escape, the smoke the sign of their destruction, the trap sprung, the foe caught, vv.19); the men of Ai seeing the smoke and trapped (the snare) — the trap (no power to flee); 'the people that fled to the wilderness turned back upon the pursuers' (Israel turning to fight, the snare closed, the figure, the ruin that closes on the doomed, the providence, the trap, the men of Ai cut off between the two forces to follow, vv.21-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when the men of Ai looked behind them, they saw, and, behold, the smoke of the city ascended up to heaven, and they had no power to flee this way or that way: and the people that fled to the wilderness turned back upon the pursuers
the men of Ai seeing the smoke and trapped, with Israel turning back
the men of Ai caught between the burning city and the turning army, the snare closed
no way of escape

schools.summary: Joshua and Israel turning to slay the men of Ai: 'and when Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city, and that the smoke of the city ascended, then they turned again, and slew the men of Ai' — seeing the smoke, Joshua and Israel turned and fell on the men of Ai (the and when Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city, and that the smoke of the city ascended, the signal seen, the then they turned again, and slew the men of Ai, turning to slay, the figure, the main force turning at the sign to fall on the trapped foe, the truth that the feigned flight became a real attack, the foe caught, the figure of the turn from flight to victory, the trap closing, the smoke the sign for the turn, the foe slain between the forces, vv.20); the smoke seen as the signal (the sign) — the signal (the smoke seen); 'they turned again, and slew the men of Ai' (the main force turning to slay, the foe caught, the figure, the turn from flight to victory, the providence, the turn, the foe cut off in the midst to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city, and that the smoke of the city ascended, then they turned again, and slew the men of Ai
Joshua and Israel turning to slay the men of Ai
seeing the smoke, Joshua and Israel turned and fell on the trapped men of Ai
the feigned flight become a real attack

schools.summary: The men of Ai caught between the two forces, none escaping: 'and the other issued out of the city against them; so they were in the midst of Israel, some on this side, and some on that side: and they smote them, so that they let none of them remain or escape' — the ambush came out of the city to meet them, and the men of Ai were caught between and slain to a man (the and the other issued out of the city against them, the ambush coming out, the so they were in the midst of Israel, some on this side, and some on that side, caught between, the and they smote them, so that they let none of them remain or escape, none escaping, the figure, the foe caught between the two forces, slain to a man, the truth that the men of Ai were utterly destroyed, none left, the figure of the complete judgment that leaves none, the foe surrounded, 'they compassed me about... but in the name of the LORD will I destroy them,' Psalm 118:11, the foe slain between the forces, none escaping, the conquest complete, vv.21); the men of Ai caught between the two forces (the snare, Psalm 118:11) — the trap (in the midst); 'they smote them, so that they let none of them remain or escape' (the foe slain to a man, the complete judgment, the figure, the judgment that leaves none, the providence, the slaughter, the king of Ai taken alive to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the other issued out of the city against them; so they were in the midst of Israel, some on this side, and some on that side: and they smote them, so that they let none of them remain or escape
the men of Ai caught between the two forces, none escaping
the ambush coming out to meet them, the men of Ai caught between and slain to a man
the complete judgment that left none (Psalm 118:11)

schools.summary: The king of Ai taken alive and brought to Joshua: 'and the king of Ai they took alive, and brought him to Joshua' — the king of Ai was captured alive and brought to Joshua for judgment (the and the king of Ai they took alive, the king captured, the and brought him to Joshua, brought for judgment, the figure, the king taken alive, reserved for judgment, the chief of the foe, the truth that the leader of the doomed city was taken to be judged, the figure of the judgment that reaches the chief offender, the king brought low, the king of the city captured, the conquest complete, the foe's leader in Joshua's hand, vv.29); the king of Ai taken alive (the capture) — the capture (taken alive); 'brought him to Joshua' (the king brought for judgment, the chief reserved, the figure, the judgment that reaches the chief offender, the providence, the capture, the utter destruction of Ai's inhabitants to follow, vv.24-25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the king of Ai they took alive, and brought him to Joshua
the king of Ai taken alive and brought to Joshua
the king of Ai captured alive and brought to Joshua for judgment
the leader of the doomed city in his hand

schools.summary: The slaying of all Ai's men in the field and the return to smite the city: 'and it came to pass, when Israel had made an end of slaying all the inhabitants of Ai in the field, in the wilderness wherein they chased them, and when they were all fallen on the edge of the sword, until they were consumed, that all the Israelites returned unto Ai, and smote it with the edge of the sword' — the men of Ai were slain in the field, then Israel returned to destroy the city (the and it came to pass, when Israel had made an end of slaying all the inhabitants of Ai in the field, in the wilderness wherein they chased them, the men slain in the field, the and when they were all fallen on the edge of the sword, until they were consumed, utterly destroyed, the that all the Israelites returned unto Ai, and smote it with the edge of the sword, the city smitten, the figure, the men slain in the field and the city destroyed, the judgment complete, the truth that the foe was consumed and the city smitten, the ban fulfilled, the figure of the thorough judgment that leaves nothing, the city and people destroyed, the conquest accomplished, the ban carried out, the destruction of Ai, vv.22); the men of Ai slain in the field (the destruction) — the slaughter (in the field); 'all the Israelites returned unto Ai, and smote it with the edge of the sword' (the city smitten, the ban fulfilled, the figure, the thorough judgment that leaves nothing, the providence, the destruction, the number of the slain to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And it came to pass, when Israel had made an end of slaying all the inhabitants of Ai in the field, in the wilderness wherein they chased them, and when they were all fallen on the edge of the sword, until they were consumed, that all the Israelites returned unto Ai, and smote it with the edge of the sword
the slaying of all Ai's men in the field and the return to smite the city
the men of Ai slain in the field and the city destroyed
the thorough judgment that left nothing

schools.summary: The twelve thousand of Ai that fell that day: 'and so it was, that all that fell that day, both of men and women, were twelve thousand, even all the men of Ai' — twelve thousand, the whole population of Ai, fell that day (the and so it was, that all that fell that day, both of men and women, were twelve thousand, the number, the even all the men of Ai, all of them, the figure, the whole city, twelve thousand, destroyed, the judgment complete, the truth that all the people of Ai perished, none left, the figure of the completeness of the judgment on the doomed city, the whole consumed, the number that marks the totality of the destruction, the conquest of Ai accomplished, vv.24); twelve thousand of Ai fallen (the destruction) — the number (twelve thousand); 'even all the men of Ai' (all the people of Ai destroyed, the judgment complete, the figure, the completeness of the judgment, the providence, the number, Joshua keeping the spear stretched out till all was done to follow, vv.26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And so it was, that all that fell that day, both of men and women, were twelve thousand, even all the men of Ai
the twelve thousand of Ai that fell that day
twelve thousand, the whole population of Ai, destroyed that day
the judgment complete and none left

schools.summary: That Joshua drew not back his outstretched hand till all Ai was destroyed: 'for Joshua drew not his hand back, wherewith he stretched out the spear, until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai' — the persevering hand — 'for Joshua drew not his hand back, wherewith he stretched out the spear, until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai': Joshua kept his hand stretched out, holding the spear, until the whole work of judgment was accomplished (the witnesses noting the significance — the perseverance of the leader's act of faith, held out unbroken till the work was done, the spear kept stretched out, the act of faith maintained to the end, the victory secured by the unwearied hand, the lesson — that the faith and prayer that begin a work must persevere till it is finished, not failing or slackening, the hand held out till the end, as Moses's hands were stayed up till Amalek was overcome, the truth that the leader's persevering faith is the channel of God's continued power, the work not done by a moment's act but by faith held to the end, 'when Moses held up his hand... and his hands were steady until the going down of the sun,' Exodus 17:11-12, the figure — the perseverance of faith and prayer, the believer enduring to the end, 'he that shall endure unto the end, the same shall be saved,' Matthew 24:13, the truth that God's victories are won by faith that does not let go, the hand not drawn back till the conquest is complete, the figure of the unwearied intercession and faith that holds till God has finished his work, the call to persevere in faith and prayer to the end of the work, the warning that the hand drawn back too soon loses the victory, the figure of the perseverance of faith that holds out till the work of God is finished); Joshua keeping his hand stretched out with the spear unbroken, the act of faith maintained as Moses's hands at Amalek (the persevering hand, Exodus 17:11-12) — the perseverance (hand not drawn back); 'until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai' (the hand held till the whole work was done, the perseverance of faith that endures to the end, the victory secured by the unwearied hand, the faith that endures to the end, Matthew 24:13) — the figure (the perseverance of faith and prayer that holds till God's work is finished, the providence, the perseverance, the spoil and the burning of Ai to follow, vv.27-28) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Joshua's hand held out till the work was done signify?

sides.position: The perseverance of faith that holds out till the work of God is finished - the leader's act of faith maintained unbroken till the conquest was complete, as Moses's hands were stayed up till Amalek was overcome (Exodus 17:11-12), a figure of the perseverance of faith and prayer and the believer enduring to the end (Matthew 24:13), the victory won by the hand not drawn back

themes: For Joshua drew not his hand back, wherewith he stretched out the spear, until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai
that Joshua drew not back his outstretched hand till all Ai was destroyed (the crux of the perseverance of faith that holds out till the work of God is finished)
the perseverance of the leader's act of faith, held out unbroken till the work was done, the faith that begins a work persevering till it is finished
as Moses's hands stayed up till Amalek was overcome, a figure of the faith and prayer that endure to the end (Exodus 17:11-12; Matthew 24:13)

schools.summary: The spoil and cattle of Ai taken as God had allowed: 'only the cattle and the spoil of that city Israel took for a prey unto themselves, according unto the word of the LORD which he commanded Joshua' — Israel took the spoil and cattle of Ai, as God had permitted, unlike Jericho (the only the cattle and the spoil of that city Israel took for a prey unto themselves, the spoil taken, the according unto the word of the LORD which he commanded Joshua, as God allowed, the figure, the spoil lawfully taken, as God allowed, where Achan had sinned, the truth that what was forbidden at Jericho was allowed at Ai, by God's word, the figure of the lawful enjoyment of what God grants, the spoil now permitted, the contrast with Achan's theft of the devoted thing, the obedience that takes only what God allows, vv.2); the spoil and cattle taken (the prey) — the spoil (taken); 'according unto the word of the LORD which he commanded Joshua' (the spoil taken as God allowed, the lawful enjoyment, the figure, the lawful enjoyment of what God grants, the providence, the spoil, the burning of Ai to a heap to follow, vv.28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Only the cattle and the spoil of that city Israel took for a prey unto themselves, according unto the word of the LORD which he commanded Joshua
the spoil and cattle of Ai taken as God had allowed
Israel taking the spoil and cattle of Ai as God had permitted, the lawful enjoyment of what God grants
in contrast with Achan's theft of the devoted thing

schools.summary: Joshua burning Ai and making it a perpetual heap: 'and Joshua burnt Ai, and made it an heap for ever, even a desolation unto this day' — Joshua burnt Ai and left it a perpetual ruin (the and Joshua burnt Ai, the city burnt, the and made it an heap for ever, even a desolation unto this day, a perpetual ruin, the figure, the city made a heap for ever, the lasting witness of judgment, the truth that Ai was left a perpetual desolation, the judgment abiding, the figure of the ruin that remains as a witness against sin, 'an heap for ever,' the city consumed, the desolation lasting, the judgment that endures, vv.26); Joshua burning the city (the judgment) — the burning (Ai burnt); 'made it an heap for ever, even a desolation unto this day' (the city a perpetual ruin, the lasting witness, the figure, the ruin that remains as a witness against sin, the providence, the desolation, the king of Ai hanged on a tree to follow, vv.29) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joshua burnt Ai, and made it an heap for ever, even a desolation unto this day
Joshua burning Ai and making it a perpetual heap
Joshua burning Ai and leaving it a perpetual ruin, the lasting witness of judgment
against the doomed city

schools.summary: The king of Ai hanged till evening, then buried under a heap of stones: 'and the king of Ai he hanged on a tree until eventide: and as soon as the sun was down, Joshua commanded that they should take his carcase down from the tree, and cast it at the entering of the gate of the city, and raise thereon a great heap of stones, that remaineth unto this day' — the king was hanged till evening, then taken down and buried under a heap of stones, as the law required (the and the king of Ai he hanged on a tree until eventide, the king hanged, the and as soon as the sun was down, Joshua commanded that they should take his carcase down from the tree, taken down at evening, the and cast it at the entering of the gate... and raise thereon a great heap of stones, buried under stones, the figure, the king hanged and taken down by evening, as the law required, the judgment and the obedience to the law, the truth that even in judgment Joshua kept the law, the body not left on the tree overnight, 'his body shall not remain all night upon the tree,' Deuteronomy 21:23, the figure of the curse of the hanged, taken down by evening, the law honoured, the heap of stones a memorial of judgment, the obedience that judges by the law, vv.28); the king taken down by evening, as the law required (the law, Deuteronomy 21:23) — the law kept (taken down at evening); 'raise thereon a great heap of stones, that remaineth unto this day' (the heap of stones a memorial of judgment, the witness, the figure, the curse of the hanged, the law honoured, the providence, the judgment, the altar built at Ebal to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the king of Ai he hanged on a tree until eventide: and as soon as the sun was down, Joshua commanded that they should take his carcase down from the tree, and cast it at the entering of the gate of the city, and raise thereon a great heap of stones, that remaineth unto this day
the king of Ai hanged till evening, then buried under a heap of stones
the king hanged and taken down by evening as the law required, buried under a heap of stones, a memorial of judgment
the law kept even in the conquest (Deuteronomy 21:23)

schools.summary: That Joshua built an altar to the LORD in mount Ebal: 'then Joshua built an altar unto the LORD God of Israel in mount Ebal' — the covenant altar — 'then Joshua built an altar unto the LORD God of Israel in mount Ebal': Joshua, having entered the land, built an altar at Ebal as Moses had commanded, to renew the covenant and worship God (the witnesses noting the significance — the establishment of the worship of God in the land at the appointed place, the covenant renewed by sacrifice, the altar at Ebal, the mount of the curse, where the law commanded the altar and the writing of the law, the worship of God set up in the land at the very outset of the conquest, the lesson — that the first care of God's people in the land was the worship of God and the renewal of his covenant, the altar before the conquest was finished, the priority of worship, the covenant renewed by sacrifice at the appointed place, as Moses commanded, Deuteronomy 27:4-5, the truth that the people of God are to set up his worship and own his covenant wherever he plants them, 'in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee,' Exodus 20:24, the figure — the altar of sacrifice the place of meeting with God, the covenant sealed in blood, fulfilled in the one altar and sacrifice of Christ, 'we have an altar,' Hebrews 13:10, the truth that the entrance into the inheritance is sealed by worship and covenant, the people consecrated to God, the figure of the worship of God established at the first, the altar the centre of the people's life, the call to make the worship of God the first concern, the warning that the land enjoyed without the altar is forfeited, the figure of the covenant worship of God established in the land at the appointed place); Joshua building an altar to the LORD as the first act of worship in the land, the covenant renewed by sacrifice (the worship established, Exodus 20:24) — the altar (built to the LORD); 'in mount Ebal' (the altar at Ebal, the place Moses had commanded, the mount of the curse where the covenant was renewed and the law written, the worship set up at the appointed place, the covenant renewed by sacrifice, Deuteronomy 27:4-5; Hebrews 13:10) — the figure (the altar of sacrifice fulfilled in the one altar of Christ, the providence, the altar, the unhewn stones and the offerings to follow, vv.31) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Joshua's altar at Ebal signify?

sides.position: The covenant worship of God established in the land at the appointed place - the worship of God set up at the very outset of the conquest, the covenant renewed by sacrifice as Moses commanded (Deuteronomy 27:4-5), the first care of God's people in the land (Exodus 20:24), a figure of the altar of sacrifice fulfilled in the one altar and sacrifice of Christ (Hebrews 13:10)

themes: Then Joshua built an altar unto the LORD God of Israel in mount Ebal
that Joshua built an altar to the LORD in mount Ebal (the crux of the covenant worship of God established in the land at the appointed place)
the worship of God set up at the appointed place at the very outset of the conquest, the covenant renewed by sacrifice as Moses commanded, the first care of God's people in the land
a figure of the altar of sacrifice fulfilled in the one altar and sacrifice of Christ (Deuteronomy 27:4-5; Exodus 20:24; Hebrews 13:10)

schools.summary: The altar of whole stones, untouched by iron, on which they offered burnt and peace offerings: 'as Moses the servant of the LORD commanded the children of Israel, as it is written in the book of the law of Moses, an altar of whole stones, over which no man hath lift up any iron: and they offered thereon burnt offerings unto the LORD, and sacrificed peace offerings' — the unhewn-stone altar — 'as Moses the servant of the LORD commanded... an altar of whole stones, over which no man hath lift up any iron: and they offered thereon burnt offerings unto the LORD, and sacrificed peace offerings': the altar was of whole, unhewn stones, untouched by iron, and on it were offered burnt and peace offerings (the witnesses noting the significance — the worship of God according to his own command, the altar of natural stones unadorned by man's art, the law forbidding the lifting up of iron on the altar, lest man's tool pollute it, the worship simple and as God appointed, Exodus 20:25, the lesson — that God is to be worshipped as he commands, not as man devises, the altar of his appointment, not of human improvement, the unhewn stones a rebuke to the pride of man's art, the worship pure and simple, the truth that the offerings — burnt offerings for atonement and peace offerings for communion — were the appointed means of approach to God, the covenant sealed in sacrifice, 'ye shall offer your oblations... a sacrifice of peace offerings,' Leviticus 17:5, the figure — the altar and offerings fulfilled in Christ, the burnt offering of his entire devotion and the peace offering of reconciliation, 'having made peace through the blood of his cross,' Colossians 1:20, the truth that the worship of God rests on his appointment and on sacrifice, not on the adornment of man, the figure of the simplicity and purity of true worship, the altar of God's making, the call to worship God as he commands, the warning against the will-worship that adds man's art to God's appointment, the figure of the worship of God on his own terms, the altar unadorned by man's art and the offerings of atonement and communion); the altar of whole, unhewn stones untouched by iron, as the law commanded, the worship unadorned by man's art (the altar of God's appointment, Exodus 20:25) — the altar (of whole stones); 'and they offered thereon burnt offerings unto the LORD, and sacrificed peace offerings' (the burnt offerings of atonement and the peace offerings of communion, the appointed means of approach to God, fulfilled in Christ the burnt and peace offering, the covenant sealed in sacrifice, Leviticus 17:5; Colossians 1:20) — the figure (the offerings fulfilled in Christ's sacrifice, the providence, the offerings, the writing of the law on the stones to follow, vv.32) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the altar of unhewn stones, untouched by iron, establish?

sides.position: The worship of God on his own terms, unadorned by man's art - the altar of natural stones as the law commanded, the iron forbidden lest man's tool pollute it (Exodus 20:25), the offerings of atonement (burnt) and communion (peace) the appointed means of approach (Leviticus 17:5), a figure of the altar and offerings fulfilled in Christ, the burnt offering of his devotion and the peace offering of reconciliation (Colossians 1:20)

themes: As Moses the servant of the LORD commanded the children of Israel, as it is written in the book of the law of Moses, an altar of whole stones, over which no man hath lift up any iron: and they offered thereon burnt offerings unto the LORD, and sacrificed peace offerings
the altar of whole stones, untouched by iron, on which they offered burnt and peace offerings (the crux of the worship of God on his own terms, unadorned by man's art)
the worship of God according to his command, the altar of natural stones unadorned by man's art, the iron forbidden lest man's tool pollute it, the offerings of atonement and communion
a figure of the altar and offerings fulfilled in Christ, the burnt offering of his devotion and the peace offering of reconciliation (Exodus 20:25; Leviticus 17:5; Colossians 1:20)

schools.summary: Joshua writing a copy of the law on the stones: 'and he wrote there upon the stones a copy of the law of Moses, which he wrote in the presence of the children of Israel' — Joshua wrote a copy of the law on the stones before all the people, as Moses commanded (the and he wrote there upon the stones a copy of the law of Moses, the law written on the stones, the which he wrote in the presence of the children of Israel, before all the people, the figure, the law written on the stones, the covenant published before all, as Moses commanded, the truth that the law was set up in writing in the land, the covenant owned, the figure of the law published and set before the people, the word made plain, 'write the vision, and make it plain upon tables,' Habakkuk 2:2, the law inscribed at the entrance into the land, the covenant renewed, the word set before all, vv.30); the law written on the stones (the publication, Habakkuk 2:2) — the writing (the law on the stones); 'which he wrote in the presence of the children of Israel' (the law written before all the people, the covenant published, the figure, the word made plain before all, the providence, the writing, the people set for the blessing and the curse to follow, vv.33) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he wrote there upon the stones a copy of the law of Moses, which he wrote in the presence of the children of Israel
Joshua writing a copy of the law on the stones
Joshua writing a copy of the law on the stones before all the people, the covenant published and the word set before them
at the entrance into the land (Habakkuk 2:2)

schools.summary: All Israel set before Gerizim and Ebal for the blessing: 'and all Israel, and their elders, and officers, and their judges, stood on this side the ark and on that side before the priests the Levites, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, as well the stranger, as he that was born among them; half of them over against mount Gerizim, and half of them over against mount Ebal; as Moses the servant of the LORD had commanded before, that they should bless the people of Israel' — the whole nation, with the stranger, stood divided before the two mounts for the blessing and the curse (the and all Israel, and their elders, and officers, and their judges, stood on this side the ark and on that side, the whole nation arrayed, the as well the stranger, as he that was born among them, the stranger included, the half of them over against mount Gerizim, and half of them over against mount Ebal, divided before the mounts, the as Moses the servant of the LORD had commanded before, that they should bless the people of Israel, as Moses commanded, the figure, the whole people, stranger and native, arrayed before the mounts of blessing and curse, the truth that all Israel, even the stranger, took part in the covenant renewal, the figure of the covenant set before all, the blessing and the curse, 'I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing,' Deuteronomy 30:19, the people divided before Gerizim and Ebal, the covenant solemnized, the assembly for the blessing and curse, vv.34); the whole nation, with the stranger, arrayed before the ark (the covenant assembly, Deuteronomy 30:19) — the assembly (all Israel); 'half of them over against mount Gerizim, and half of them over against mount Ebal... that they should bless the people of Israel' (the people divided before the mounts of blessing and curse, the covenant solemnized, the figure, the covenant set before all, the providence, the assembly, the reading of the blessings and curses to follow, vv.34-35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And all Israel, and their elders, and officers, and their judges, stood on this side the ark and on that side before the priests the Levites, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, as well the stranger, as he that was born among them; half of them over against mount Gerizim, and half of them over against mount Ebal; as Moses the servant of the LORD had commanded before, that they should bless the people of Israel
all Israel set before Gerizim and Ebal for the blessing
the whole nation, with the stranger, arrayed before the two mounts of blessing and curse
the covenant solemnized as Moses commanded (Deuteronomy 30:19)

schools.summary: That Joshua read all the words of the law, the blessings and the curses: 'and afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law' — the whole law proclaimed — 'and afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law': Joshua read the whole law, both its blessings and its curses, exactly as written, before the assembled nation (the witnesses noting the significance — the public proclamation of the entire word of God, nothing omitted, with both its promises and its threatenings, the law read in full, the blessings on obedience and the curses on disobedience, the covenant set before the people in its wholeness, the lesson — that the whole counsel of God is to be declared, not the blessings only nor the curses only, but all the words of the law, the comfort and the warning together, the integrity of the proclamation, 'all that is written,' nothing kept back, the truth that the minister of God must declare the whole counsel of God, 'I have not shunned to declare unto you all the counsel of God,' Acts 20:27, the figure — the faithful preaching that sets before men life and death, blessing and curse, the law and the gospel in their fulness, 'preach the word... reprove, rebuke, exhort,' 2 Timothy 4:2, the truth that the word with its sanctions is to be read and heard by all, the covenant published whole, the figure of the law and gospel proclaimed entire, the people warned and encouraged, the call to declare all God's word, withholding nothing, the warning against a partial gospel that omits the curse or the blessing, the figure of the public proclamation of the whole word of God, with its sanctions, before all the people); Joshua reading the entire law before the people, nothing omitted, the whole counsel declared (the integrity of the proclamation, Acts 20:27) — the reading (all the words); 'the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law' (the blessings on obedience and the curses on disobedience both proclaimed, exactly as written, the covenant set before the people whole, as the faithful preaching that declares all God's word, the whole counsel, life and death, 2 Timothy 4:2) — the figure (the law and gospel proclaimed entire, the people warned and encouraged, the providence, the reading, the whole congregation hearing every word to follow, vv.35) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the reading of all the law, blessings and curses, establish?

sides.position: The public proclamation of the whole word of God, with its sanctions, before all the people - the entire word read, nothing omitted, both blessings and curses, the whole counsel of God declared, not the blessings only nor the curses only (Acts 20:27), a figure of the faithful preaching that sets before men life and death, the law and gospel in their fulness (2 Timothy 4:2), the covenant published whole

themes: And afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law
that Joshua read all the words of the law, the blessings and the curses (the crux of the public proclamation of the whole word of God, with its sanctions, before all the people)
the public proclamation of the entire word of God, nothing omitted, with both its promises and its threatenings, the whole counsel of God declared
a figure of the faithful preaching that sets before men life and death, the law and gospel in their fulness (Acts 20:27; 2 Timothy 4:2)

schools.summary: That Joshua read every word before all, including women, children, and strangers: 'there was not a word of all that Moses commanded, which Joshua read not before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers that were conversant among them' — Joshua read every word of the law before the whole assembly, none excluded (the there was not a word of all that Moses commanded, which Joshua read not, every word read, the before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers that were conversant among them, before all, none excluded, the figure, every word read, the whole word before the whole people, none left out, the truth that the entire law was proclaimed to all, women, children, and strangers included, the figure of the word for all the people, none excluded from hearing, 'gather the people... that they may hear, and that they may learn,' Deuteronomy 31:12, the completeness of the reading and of the audience, the whole word to the whole people, vv.34); every word of the law read, none omitted (the integrity, Deuteronomy 31:12) — the completeness (not a word omitted); 'before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers' (the whole people, none excluded, hearing the word, the word for all, the figure, the word for all the people, the providence, the completeness, the conquest of the southern cities to follow, ch.9-10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: There was not a word of all that Moses commanded, which Joshua read not before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers that were conversant among them
that Joshua read every word before all, including women, children, and strangers
Joshua reading every word of the law before the whole assembly, none excluded
the entire word proclaimed to all the people (Deuteronomy 31:12)
